pathophysiology_summary: A known severe peanut allergy with accidental ingestion followed rapidly by throat tightness, stridorous/noisy breathing, difficulty speaking, generalised urticaria, lip swelling, and lightheadedness constitutes anaphylaxis — a life-threatening IgE-mediated systemic hypersensitivity reaction involving the airway, breathing, and circulation. The reported autoinjector failure (whether from expiry or technique) means the reaction is ongoing and worsening. Anaphylaxis is a time-critical emergency: prompt administration of a further dose of intramuscular adrenaline (via a second autoinjector if available) alongside immediate ambulance dispatch is the correct action, since adrenaline is the only treatment that reverses the underlying pathophysiology and antihistamines/steroids are adjuncts only. Patients remain at risk of a biphasic (recurrent) reaction hours after apparent initial improvement, so extended observation in hospital is required regardless of early response.

differential.evidence_basis: Known severe allergen ingestion with throat tightness and hives within minutes.

differential.diagnosis: Isolated urticaria/allergic skin reaction without systemic involvement
differential.evidence_basis: Hives alone without airway/breathing/circulatory involvement would be a milder allergic reaction, but this is excluded once respiratory and circulatory symptoms are confirmed.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Airway symptoms (throat tightness, voice change) alongside known allergen exposure should immediately place anaphylaxis as leading and trigger emergency assessment.

differential.diagnosis: Anaphylaxis with inadequate initial adrenaline response
differential.evidence_basis: Persistent/worsening airway and circulatory symptoms despite one autoinjector use confirm ongoing severe reaction requiring a further adrenaline dose.

differential_progression.scoring_note: By this stage, use of a second adrenaline autoinjector alongside continued emergency escalation should be clearly recommended.

differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis requiring further adrenaline, emergency transport, and extended hospital observation for biphasic reaction risk.

differential_progression.scoring_note: A plan lacking both a second adrenaline dose recommendation and 000 dispatch is critically inadequate.

red_flags.description: Known severe allergen ingestion with rapid symptom onset
red_flags.clinical_significance: Establishes high pre-test probability of anaphylaxis.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Delayed recognition of a life-threatening allergic reaction.

red_flags.description: Throat tightness, stridorous breathing, and difficulty speaking
red_flags.clinical_significance: Indicates upper airway compromise, a hallmark of severe anaphylaxis and imminent airway obstruction risk.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progression to complete airway obstruction and respiratory arrest.

red_flags.description: Measured hypotension (BP 90/55), audible wheeze and stridor on examination (clinician-only findings)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Confirms objective severity of the anaphylactic reaction.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not directly available to the AI Doctor via chat.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Withholding a second adrenaline dose due to the first device 'not working'
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: AI Doctor might hesitate to recommend a second autoinjector dose out of caution about 'too much adrenaline' — this is incorrect; ongoing/worsening anaphylaxis after an ineffective or expired first dose warrants prompt repeat dosing while awaiting paramedics.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Direct visualisation of airway swelling/stridor severity
Oxygen saturation and blood pressure measurement
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Immediate 000 ambulance dispatch; advise use of a second adrenaline autoinjector now given ongoing airway and circulatory symptoms
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Any hesitation to recommend repeat adrenaline dosing risks progression to complete airway obstruction or cardiovascular collapse.
